claim.content: Cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr_{3}) undergoes crystallographic phase changes with temperature and adopts an orthorhombic ground-state structure at room temperature, yet ARPES band dispersions measured at room temperature can in practice resemble higher-symmetry cubic-phase dispersions for some momentum-space cuts; therefore using an orthorhombic-structure G_{0}W_{0} calculation that yields m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} for direct quantitative comparison with room-temperature ARPES data assumes that the orthorhombic theoretical band structure is an appropriate representation of the electronic states probed in the ARPES experiment, and further assumes that any difference in structural symmetry between the theoretical model and the actual electronic-state symmetry sampled by ARPES does not change the effective mass by an amount comparable to the experimental–theoretical difference being evaluated.
